1. Introduction
---------------

On May 5, 2023, after enduring more than three years of [public health](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/public-health "Learn more about public health from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") restrictions, the WHO declared that Covid-19 was no longer a ‘public health emergency of international concern’, thus formally marking the end to a turbulent period in modern epidemiological history. Now is therefore an opportune time to take stock and prepare for whatever the future might have in store. With global [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), future pandemics are considered more likely ([Marco Marani et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib131)). Some scholars warn that we have entered a ‘pandemic era’ ([Morens et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib138)); [Honigsbaum (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib93) even argues that we have long been in one – although he has a rather idiosyncratic definition of what constitutes a pandemic. Here we contribute to future reflections by looking backwards into the past, beyond the recent history of Covid-19. We collect and collate in one place as many lessons as we can find from reviewing the literature on all the pandemics of the Modern Era that occurred prior to the 2019 Wuhan outbreak of the novel SARS-CoV-2 virus.

Pandemics result from the emergence and spread of a novel [pathogen](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/pathogen "Learn more about pathogen from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). They differ from epidemics in that they have wider geographic spread and affect all people, and consequently are associated with a higher mortality and economic impact. Pandemics are also different from [endemic diseases](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/endemic-disease "Learn more about endemic diseases from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), the amount of disease usually present within a community, as they represent a distinct and temporary deviation from baseline morbidity and mortality (see [Doshi (2011)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib64) and [Kelly (2011)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib104) for further debate over pandemic definition). Pandemics constitute a global [catastrophic risk](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/catastrophic-risk "Learn more about catastrophic risk from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), a type of event with the potential to cause serious damage to human [wellbeing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/wellbeing "Learn more about wellbeing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") on a global scale. [Bostrom and Cirkovic (2008)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib23) set a threshold for defining these events at either an [excess mortality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/mortality "Learn more about excess mortality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") exceeding 10 million people, or economic damage more than $10 trillion.

Covid-19 was clearly a pandemic, by any definition. It also meets both of [Bostrom and Cirkovic's (2008)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib23) criteria of constituting a global [catastrophic risk](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/catastrophic-risk "Learn more about catastrophic risk from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") event. Global excess deaths for Covid-19 are estimated at 18.2 million between January 1, 2020 and December 31, 2021 ([Wang et al., 2022a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib203)). And this figure would likely have been higher were it not for the various mitigation efforts implemented globally. The [economic costs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/economic-costs "Learn more about economic costs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") associated with Covid-19 were staggering; estimated costs for the US alone amount to $16 trillion ([Cutler and Summers, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib58)). But the Covid-19 catastrophe is not completely unprecedented. The Modern Era – the two centuries since the Industrial Revolution – has seen numerous pandemic-like health crises. These potentially offer lessons on the impact of pandemics and how we can deal with them in the future.

In his survey of historical pandemics, [Kilbourne (2008)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib108) argues that ‘we must be guided by the lessons of the past, so it is essential that we reach a consensus on what these lessons are’. In this current paper we carry out the necessary spadework to do exactly that. We conduct a [systematic review](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/systematic-review "Learn more about systematic review from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of studies across all scientific fields on the topic of the impact of pandemics that took place across the 200 years prior to Covid-19. We make use of the _Web of Science_ academic journal database, a comprehensive database of high-quality peer-reviewed journal articles, as well as a secondary search of _JSTOR,_ a repository of over 2,000 academic journals which has a better coverage of publications before 1975. We select all relevant journal articles that were published before the start of the Covid-19 pandemic, to gauge what was already known about these pandemics before the most recent one emerged. We then supplement this by adding all new journal articles about historical pandemics that have been published since 2020. From this, we categorise and summarise their contents in an accessible way – and, more importantly, identify the gaps in the literature which we think should now be addressed. This review purposefully does not include literature about Covid-19 as there is still a shifting consensus which has already sparked new systematic reviews and reviews of reviews ([Wurth et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib211)).

There have been several high-quality reviews of historical pandemics published since Covid-19 emerged. These have either contextualised them within demographic and epidemiological transitions ([Shaw-Taylor, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib181)), or have focused on specific historical episodes such as the Black Death, the 1918 [Spanish Influenza](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/spanish-influenza "Learn more about Spanish Influenza from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), or modern infectious disease ([Arthi and Parman, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib10); [Beach et al., 2022a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib14); [Bloom et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib21); [Jedwab et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib95)). However, these studies have not taken explicitly multidisciplinary or interdisciplinary perspectives. Nor have these scholars undertaken _systematic_ literature reviews; these articles are written based on partial or selective sets of studies and have not undergone the rigorous search and classification process demanded of a [systematic review](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/systematic-review "Learn more about systematic review from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). (For an introduction to this methodology, see [Centre for Reviews and Dissemination (2009)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib34); for guidelines on current best practice, see [Page et al. (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib153).) Unlike other literature reviews, ours is designed to be comprehensive; we do not omit studies with low citation counts, studies in disciplines outside our own, or studies published outside high ranked field journals. The benefit of a [systematic review](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/systematic-review "Learn more about systematic review from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is that it offers a “data-led approach” to summarising the literature; rather than restricting our search to a particular class of research design or the perspective of a single discipline or journal, the [systematic review](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/systematic-review "Learn more about systematic review from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") methodology has the power to reveal to us what are considered to be the most important topics and questions being addressed across all disciplines and journals. Ultimately, a systematic review helps uncover gaps in the literature and identify possible avenues for future research ([Cooper and Hedges, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib55)).

There have long been calls for more interdisciplinary studies of historical pandemic episodes (see, e.g., [Simonsen et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib184)). Our review encompasses 169 articles from across the social and health sciences. We find the articles published in journals with a health science focus in particular offer many insightful answers to the question of the impact of historical pandemics. Such journals are not always on the radar of economists, historians and social scientists. Despite regular calls for greater interdisciplinarity, we find few truly interdisciplinary studies that engage with research across different disciplines. Each discipline instead operates within its own silo, with its own set of questions, methods, and datasets. We believe there is great scope for new cross-disciplinary work that combines – especially – demography, [epidemiology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/cohort-effect "Learn more about epidemiology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and economics with history. If brought together, this will help to break down the disciplinary divisions that at present result in scholars working in isolated parallel academic communities.

The most striking feature which stands out from our review, and the most important lesson we should take note of, is the fact that studies look at the effects of practically all pandemics in isolation, both temporally and spatially. Very few, if any, connections are made between different effects within the same pandemic, or the same effect across different pandemics. Without taking a comparative and holistic approach, we think it is very difficult to identify which pandemic context is most useful to learn from, to discern common trends across pandemics, or to understand how the demographic impact compares to the economic impact. We will be able to learn much more from historical pandemics if in future there can be a comparative aspect adopted more consistently.

Across all disciplines included in our review, the literature is dominated by studies of the 1918 [Spanish Influenza](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/spanish-influenza "Learn more about Spanish Influenza from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") pandemic and its high death toll. For example, [Smil (2012)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib185) argued that the 1918 Spanish [Influenza pandemic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/pandemic-influenza "Learn more about Influenza pandemic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was the worst in history by any standard, assumed that the death profile of future pandemics would have a similar pattern as 1918, and argued that ‘the massive mortality of people in their prime would also strain the life insurance [industry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/industry "Learn more about industry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and depress real estate values’. (Despite some debate on its naming, we consistently use the term “1918 Spanish Influenza” to refer to this pandemic, simply because this is the most used term in the literature.) Clearly, this mortality pattern was not replicated in Covid-19 pandemic, where the disease struck down the oldest most severely. Equally, there is no guarantee that the next pandemic will replicate the Covid-19 pattern. So, rather than focusing in on drawing a single line of comparison, we must therefore prepare for various scenarios by moving beyond the 1918 reference.

The most recent studies published since Covid-19 continue to focus on 1918, even though it is now widely acknowledged that policymakers ‘planned for the wrong pandemic’ ([UK Covid-19 Inquiry, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib198)). During times of crisis, policymakers tend to rely on [analogical reasoning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/analogical-reasoning "Learn more about analogical reasoning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). In the case of pandemics, the most widely known is the 1918 Spanish [Influenza pandemic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/pandemic-influenza "Learn more about Influenza pandemic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which is taught in public health classes around the world. Studies of “milder” and “phantom” pandemics are much less common, and so there is little for public health pedagogy to draw upon. The focus on 1918 is understandable given its associated death toll; however, a lower death toll does not mean the overall societal costs were low. We fear that in their laudable efforts to make the wider policymaking community aware of [worse case scenarios](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/worst-case-scenario "Learn more about worse case scenarios from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), scientists may have distorted societal views of pandemics and informed flawed policy designs.

In a similar vein, studies of the experience of developing countries during pandemics are currently rare. Our review shows articles which include such countries often find their experiences differed from those of more developed regions. The historical data available for developing countries are constantly improving, so there are opportunities available now for future scholars to take existing study designs of pandemics for developed country locations and replicate them for the developing world.

The development of Covid-19 has highlighted the fact policymakers were inadequately prepared, despite the relative frequency with which pandemics and pandemic-like events have occurred. Academic historians traditionally maintain the view that we cannot learn direct lessons from the past because is too easy to ‘pick and choose what you want’ and justify practically anything by appealing to one or other interpretation of past events ([Colvin and Winfree, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib54)). We challenge this view and align ourselves instead with the "New Applied History" movement described in [MacMillan (2008)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib127). We believe that historical episodes _do_ contain useful knowledge – knowledge that can help us to better understand the present. The past has already offered many policy lessons for current issues, such as the 1930s Great Depression during the 2008 Global Financial Crisis ([Hetzel, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib91); [James, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib94)). By revisiting past pandemics, policymakers can see what lessons they offer for both now and the future. As we demonstrate in this review, we are not starting from scratch.

2. Methods
----------

### 2.1. Search criteria

We followed the standard [systematic review](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/systematic-review "Learn more about systematic review from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") process ([Centre for Reviews and Dissemination, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib34); [Page et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#bib153)). The aim of our systematic search was to identify research papers which discuss the _impact_ of pandemics, namely their demographic or economic impact. We only include full research articles, not journal publications such as letters, reviews, or conference abstracts. We had three phases to our literature selection process which was carried out between June 2020 and September 2023. The main search was carried out using _Web of Science_. We searched all papers containing the term “pandemic” in either their title or abstract. This search was then supplemented by searches of _JSTOR_ to accommodate papers published prior to 1975. We then examined the [bibliographies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/bibliographies "Learn more about bibliographies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of returned papers for any articles missing from our search. We also carried out a search of pre-print working paper series, following through to see what papers were subsequently peer-reviewed and published prior to September 2023, to capture the developments in research that have been carried out in the wake of the Covid-19 pandemic. Our criteria for “historical” are studies of past events, so we make a distinction between contemporary observations that are descriptive in nature and studies that are backward-looking and analytical. (For example, contemporary coverage of the 1890 pandemic in British medical journals was descriptive.) [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0277953623008912#fig1) outlines the overall process of the [systematic review](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/systematic-review "Learn more about systematic review from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

enriched_points.expanded_content: A pandemic is a global outbreak of an infectious disease, characterized by rapid spread across countries and continents, significantly affecting a large portion of the population.
Pandemics have profoundly influenced human history, shaping societal structures, economies, and cultures, often resulting in significant mortality and altering the course of civilizations.
Pandemics cause widespread health crises, strain healthcare systems, disrupt economies, and can lead to social unrest, fundamentally changing daily life and interactions within communities.
Understanding pandemics enhances preparedness, informs public health policies, and improves response strategies, ultimately reducing the impact of future outbreaks on global health.
This presentation aims to explore the history of pandemics, their societal impacts, and lessons learned to improve future pandemic preparedness and response efforts.
